entity_facts: ["Yuki Tanaka has been a cartographer obsessed with historic shipwrecks for most of her adult life. She wakes at 5:00 a.m. to brew sencha and sketch outlines before the day gets noisy. Yuki paints sea-floor cross-sections in indigo watercolor on linen and numbers every coordinate with a faded fountain-pen ink. She collects dented brass sextants and keeps them in a cedar box on the highest shelf. From every wreck site she has a tiny jar of sand labeled with date and tide, and the jars line a window ledge like a slow geological chronicle. Yuki refuses to rely on GPS when mapping; her maps are hand-ruled on paper grids and finished with hand-stitched cloth tags for depth notes. She will not fly and prefers to travel by ferry whenever a site requires crossing open water. There is a vintage diving helmet on her bookshelf that she polishes on Sundays while she reads maritime logs. Yuki has a hard rule: she never goes into fieldwork without a diver's log signed and a local salvager's permit. Also, she is severely allergic to shellfish, so any boat picnic around a survey is strictly shellfish-free. Friends joke that she treats each map like an archaeological diary rather than a quick GPS export."]

entity_facts: ["Felix Andersen is an amateur astronomer who will complain about city light pollution at any dinner party. He lives in a weathered cabin well outside town and drives two hours to the same dark-sky reserve on new-moon nights. Felix owns a 10-inch Dobsonian telescope that he insists on hauling by hand rather than using a motorized mount. He refuses to use phone apps when observing and prefers paper star charts folded into the back pocket of his patched navy parka. Every Sunday he polishes the Dobson's mirrors and checks the collimation like it is a ritual. Felix carries a thermos of chamomile tea to keep his hands from getting shaky when the temperature drops. He volunteers with a local group that campaigns for shielded streetlamps and has a cardboard portfolio of letters to city council. Felix keeps meteorite fragments in labeled tins and enjoys passing them around for kids at outreach nights. He never attends rooftop stargazing events inside the city because the light and noise drive him nuts. On rare clear winter mornings he will trade sleep for a dawn session to watch Jupiter rise."]

entity_facts: ["Nia Williams is an amateur astronomer who hates city light pollution and works hard to avoid it. She keeps a portable 8-inch Schmidt-Cassegrain that folds into a foam-lined case for nights out. Instead of driving, Nia rides a foldable electric bike to the outskirts and parks by her aunt's farmhouse field for the best horizon views. She never observes without a pair of thermal gloves stashed in the saddlebag and a spare pair in case temperatures nosedive. Nia likes to shoot a roll of black-and-white film for long-exposure astrophotography; she still develops the negatives at home in a bathroom darkroom. Her observing notes live in a leather-bound logbook where she sketches star fields and annotates seeing conditions. She brings spiced chai in a solar-heated flask and always labels her shots with the exact local sidereal time. Nia volunteers at the local planetarium teaching kids how to find constellations with just their eyes. She has a rule not to use gasoline cars on observing nights whenever possible and prefers the quiet route along dirt lanes. People who join her know to expect careful preparation rather than last-minute runs into the lighted city."]

entity_facts: ["Hana Sato is an architect who specializes in adaptive reuse of old factories and loves the smell of oil-stained brick. She starts every day sketching at 6:00 a.m., coffee in hand, before the emails pile up. Hana commutes by tram to her studio and prefers public transit to driving because it keeps her connected to the city fabric. She keeps a 1920s blueprint trunk in the corner and a battered box of reclaimed wood samples labeled by grain and provenance. Hana has a hard rule to preserve at least one original machine in every major renovation; it is part ethics and part storytelling. She also refuses to remove original industrial windows and will design around them rather than replace them. On the side she makes matchstick scale models of factory floors and glues in tiny strips of metal for the mezzanines. Community consultation is non-negotiable for her projects, and she will not demolish without full documentation and a salvage plan. Hana always wears steel-toe boots on site and brings a tiny notebook for quick elevation sketches. Her espresso order is always the same: short with oat milk, no sugar."]

entity_facts: ["Elena Rossi balances life as a part-time pastry chef and a mathematician who writes proofs on her kitchen chalkboard. She wakes at 3:00 a.m. and is usually laminating dough by 4:00 a.m. when the bakery needs croissants. Elena hand-laminates all her pastries and refuses to use electric mixers for butter layers; precision is everything. She uses a scale that measures to 0.1 grams and annotates recipes with margin notes that look suspiciously like lemmas. Elena is lactose-intolerant but tolerates clarified butter, so most laminated doughs use ghee rather than straight cream. Her signature is a pistachio croissant finished with a thin lemon glaze, and she always wears a flour-dusted navy apron while baking. After the bakery closes she retreats to the chalkboard to work on a combinatorics proof and claims to write at least one lemma per pastry batch. Elena keeps a set of numbered ceramic ramekins\u2014there are twenty-three of them\u2014and a notebook where recipes and proofs sit side by side. She refuses to rush a lamination; if the dough hasn't rested the exact amount of time she won't bake it."]
